topic: Sharp partial identification of the marginal treatment effect curve and policy-relevant treatment effects with two or more binary instruments, overlapping but distinct propensity-score supports, and a flexible MTE shape class (Bernstein-polynomial basis): a closed-form linear program characterizing the identified set as a function of the joint instrument-propensity distribution, with an explicit dual representation that yields PRTE bounds strictly tighter than the single-instrument MST (2018, Section 6) benchmark by a computable amount equal to the moment-cone width of the joint propensity distribution projected onto the basis; recovers Mogstad-Santos-Torgovitsky (2018, ECMA) Theorem 1 in the single-instrument special case. MST (2018, Section 6, p. 1623) explicitly state the multi-instrument extension is left for future work.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize sharp PRTE bounds for a Bernstein-basis MTE class with two or more binary instruments as a closed-form primal-dual LP whose tightening over the best single-instrument MST bound equals a computable projected moment-cone width.

source_refs.claim: MST express IV-like estimands and policy parameters as weighted averages of MTE/MTR functions and use Bernstein-polynomial LP bounds; their Section 6 future-work discussion leaves the multi-instrument extension open.
source_refs.author: Magne Mogstad, Andres Santos, Alexander Torgovitsky

source_refs.claim: Develops a multiple-IV MTE framework under partial monotonicity and mutual-consistency constraints, showing that information from multiple instruments can be aggregated more informatively than using each instrument separately.
source_refs.author: Magne Mogstad, Alexander Torgovitsky, Christopher R. Walters

source_refs.claim: Provides sharp nonparametric LP bounds for policy-relevant weighted averages of the MTE when instruments are binary, but does not give the projected moment-cone width comparison to a single-IV MST benchmark.
source_refs.author: Sukjin Han, Shenshen Yang

source_refs.excerpt: a precise non-pasting theorem for local dynamic IV certificates with a sharp primal/dual witness
subfield LP specialization, overclaimed via generic LP-duality

open_problems.why_unsolved: MST provides the single-instrument LP/Bernstein benchmark and the topic-specified future-work hook, while MTW 2024 shows that multiple instruments can be aggregated through partial monotonicity and mutual consistency. The remaining gap is not generic LP duality: the missing object is an algebraic comparison theorem that maps the joint binary-instrument propensity distribution into a projected moment cone and proves that the joint-support PRTE interval is tighter than the best single-IV interval by exactly that cone-width term. Prior CausalSmith banked IV LP attempts warn that finite latent-type sharpness without a new primal/dual witness is too generic.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem giving a finite-dimensional primal LP for Bernstein MTE coefficients, its dual support-function representation, a nonnegative projected moment-cone width formula for strict PRTE tightening, and the single-instrument MST limit as a corollary.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct uniformly valid inference for the scalar gain by which joint-support multi-IV PRTE bounds improve over the minimum single-IV MST-style bound.

source_refs.claim: The multiple-IV MTE framework aggregates instruments through mutual consistency, but its target is the identified set rather than inference on a scalar gain over single-IV bounds.
source_refs.author: Magne Mogstad, Alexander Torgovitsky, Christopher R. Walters

source_refs.claim: Develops PRTE inference with orthogonal scores and emphasizes the role of preliminary nuisance estimates, but not LP-bound improvement functionals.
source_refs.author: Yuya Sasaki, Takuya Ura

source_refs.claim: Provides uniformly valid inference for conditional moment inequalities, a natural but not yet specialized tool for Bernstein-MTE LP support functions.
source_refs.author: Isaiah Andrews, Jonathan Roth, Ariel Pakes

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing PRTE inference work treats point or smooth policy-effect estimands, while modern moment-inequality inference treats broad conditional moment systems. The projected-width gain is a nonsmooth difference of support functions: the joint-IV LP value minus the best single-IV LP value. Its asymptotics can be degenerate when added joint moments are slack or when two single-IV bounds tie, so a direct transplant of PRTE orthogonal-score inference or generic moment-inequality confidence sets does not give a statement for the tightening amount.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A limiting-distribution or bootstrap theorem for the LP value-difference functional, with degeneracy handling at slack joint moments and ties among single-IV benchmarks, yielding confidence intervals for both PRTE bounds and the incremental tightening amount.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive sharp PRTE/MTE bounds for multiple binary instruments when partial monotonicity is allowed to fail for a bounded share of instrument-specific response types.

source_refs.claim: Shows that standard monotonicity with multiple IVs is too restrictive unless choice behavior is effectively homogeneous, motivating partial monotonicity.
source_refs.author: Magne Mogstad, Alexander Torgovitsky, Christopher R. Walters

source_refs.claim: Provides a nonparametric test for partial instrument monotonicity with multidimensional instruments, treating violations as empirically relevant diagnostics rather than a bounded-sensitivity identification problem.
source_refs.author: Hongyi Jiang, Zhenting Sun

source_refs.claim: Derives sharp MTE/PRTE bounds under imperfect IV assumptions, showing how MTE policy analysis can be layered when full validity fails.
source_refs.author: Ismael Mourifie, Yuanyuan Wan

open_problems.why_unsolved: MTW 2021/2024 replace impossible global monotonicity with partial monotonicity, and Jiang-Sun test whether that condition holds. The study-pipeline defier gaps are marked all_failed, so their framing must be treated as a hypothesis rather than as established substrate. The missing identification result is a sensitivity bridge between diagnostics and policy bounds: how much a controlled mass of instrument-specific monotonicity violations can expand the multi-IV MTE/PRTE identified set relative to exact partial monotonicity.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp-bound theorem parameterized by a violation budget over response-type cells, proving primal LP constraints and a dual sensitivity formula that collapses to the partial-monotonicity multi-IV bound at zero violation and to a weaker imperfect-IV bound as the budget grows.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize when overlapping but non-nested propensity-score supports from several binary instruments identify the same policy weight as the target PRTE rather than only distinct instrument-specific LATE or MTE averages.

source_refs.claim: Establishes the MTE policy-evaluation framework and emphasizes that IV methods can fail to answer policy questions when heterogeneity enters both choices and outcomes.
source_refs.author: James J. Heckman, Edward Vytlacil

source_refs.claim: Reviews that policy relevance usually requires extrapolating from instrument-affected individuals to the population affected by the policy.
source_refs.author: Magne Mogstad, Alexander Torgovitsky

source_refs.claim: For multiple binary instruments, vector monotonicity point-identifies LATE-type parameters for groups responsive to instruments or subsets of instruments, but not a full policy-weight support equivalence theorem for PRTE bounds.
source_refs.author: Leonard Goff

source_refs.claim: Computes sharp bounds for extrapolated policy-relevant MTE averages with binary instruments, highlighting the external-validity problem for local IV evidence.
source_refs.author: Sukjin Han, Shenshen Yang

open_problems.why_unsolved: The MTE literature separates the instrument-specific complier population from the policy-relevant population, and recent binary/multiple-IV work supplies either LATE-type identified parameters or LP bounds for policy averages. What remains unresolved at the statement level is a support-comparison theorem for several binary instruments: overlapping supports can add moments, but they need not span the PRTE weight. Without a necessary-and-sufficient support condition, the proposer cannot know when the joint binary-IV design is a genuine policy-weight identification improvement rather than a tighter bound for the wrong extrapolation target.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient theorem, stated in terms of the cone generated by instrument propensity-score support weights projected onto the Bernstein basis, for when the target PRTE weight lies in the closed span or convex cone of the available multi-IV weights, together with sharp residual-bound formulas when it does not.

literature_map:
Heckman-Vytlacil 2005 Econometrica and Vytlacil 2002 Econometrica anchor the latent-index/MTE equivalence and policy-evaluation setup.
Manski-Pepper 2000 Econometrica supplies the monotone-IV/MTR partial-ID tradition, useful as a nonparametric-bounds ancestor.
Mogstad-Santos-Torgovitsky 2018 Econometrica is the direct single-instrument MST benchmark: IV-like moments, shape restrictions, Bernstein-polynomial computation, LP bounds.
MST's supplement discusses Bernstein-polynomial computational advantages for the finite-dimensional LP.
Mogstad-Torgovitsky-Walters 2021 AER identifies the core multiple-IV monotonicity problem: standard monotonicity is too restrictive with multiple distinct IVs.
Mogstad-Torgovitsky-Walters 2024 Journal of Econometrics is the closest hit: multi-IV MTE under partial monotonicity and mutual consistency.
Goff 2024 Journal of Econometrics gives a binary multi-IV vector-monotonicity LATE identification theory, adjacent but not PRTE/MTE partial-ID LP.
Han-Yang 2024 Journal of Econometrics gives sharp LP bounds for policy-relevant MTE averages with binary instruments, adjacent to the requested LP formulation.
Marx 2024 Journal of Econometrics gives analytic sharp bounds for latent-index MTE functionals using full marginal-distribution content.
Kitagawa 2021 Journal of Econometrics characterizes sharp IV identification regions under instrument independence, useful for exact-ID-region logic.
Bartalotti-Kedagni-Possebom 2023 Journal of Econometrics extends sharp MTE bounds to sample selection and discrete instruments.
Sasaki-Ura 2023 Journal of Econometrics handles PRTE estimation/inference with orthogonal scores, relevant after identification.
Mourifie-Wan 2025 Journal of Econometrics extends MTE/PRTE bounds to imperfect IV layers, distinct from multiple valid IV support aggregation.
Jiang-Sun 2023 arXiv tests partial instrument monotonicity, a validity diagnostic for the multi-IV cluster.
Andrews-Roth-Pakes 2023 Review of Economic Studies supplies modern inference tools for linear conditional moment inequalities likely needed for LP-bound inference.
prior_proposal_map:
pid_mte_multi_iv/v1 has only state and init pipeline JSONL; no qid proposal .tex or review JSONs found under doc/formalization/research.
Closest MTE-adjacent failed prior: pid_continuous_iv_no_monotonicity_v1; useful warning on correctness/novelty for propensity-derivative MTE claims.
Closest accepted IV LP artifacts: pid_dose_response_iv_v1 and pid_ordinal_late_partial_defiers_v1; both are adjacent but not multi-binary-IV MTE.
Dynamic-IV compliance v1/v2 gives reusable LP/projection language, but v1 warns generic finite latent-type LP sharpness is not enough.
Binary-IV bounded-defier envelope was rejected as generic LP-duality overclaim; current topic needs a named non-generic moment-cone width.
No bank README found that already states the exact MST Section 6 multi-instrument Bernstein-basis PRTE extension.
